did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based object detection model, specifically YOLOv10 with a PP-LCNet backbone, Multiscale Contextual Attention (MCA) neck, and Shape-IoU loss, to perform melanocytic nevus detection and melanoma screening using dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'TYPE Original Research' and presents a novel study introducing a 'reversed-exclusion strategy' using an enhanced YOLOv10 model. It details original experimental methods, including dataset construction (2,040 benign nevi and 365 melanomas), model architecture modifications (PP-LCNet, MCA, Shape-IoU), ablation studies, and comparative results against dermatologists, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: YOLOv10-based detection of melanocytic nevi: reverse exclusion optimization for melanoma screening
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma screening by proposing a 'benign-first, reverse-exclusion' strategy using an enhanced YOLOv10 object detection model. The model incorporates a PP-LCNet backbone, Multiscale Contextual Attention (MCA) neck, and Shape-IoU loss to improve detection of melanocytic nevi and exclude malignancy. It was trained on a multi-centre dermoscopic dataset of 2,040 benign nevi from three tertiary hospitals in mainland China and tested on an independent cohort of 365 biopsy-proven melanomas. The model achieved a detection mAP@0.5 of 97.69% for benign lesions and a false-negative rate of only 0.27% for melanomas. Comparative analysis showed the model's false-negative rate was significantly lower than that of dermatologists using ABCDE or 7-point checklist criteria. The results suggest this approach offers a high-precision, low-risk pathway for early melanoma screening in Chinese East-Asian clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for detection in the 'benign-first' strategy. The model was trained on 2,040 benign nevi from a multi-centre dataset and achieved a detection mAP@0.5 of 97.69% for these lesions.
Used as the high-risk category in the reverse-exclusion strategy. Tested on an independent external test set of 365 biopsy-proven melanomas to evaluate the false-negative rate (FNR) of 0.27%.

Mentioned in the introduction and references as a non-melanocytic lesion with distinguishable features and low clinical confusion rate with melanoma, but not included in the experimental dataset or model training.
Mentioned in the introduction as a non-melanocytic lesion with readily distinguishable features, but not included in the experimental dataset or model training.

all_task_types.details: The model uses YOLOv10 to detect and localize melanocytic nevi via bounding boxes. The presence of a bounding box indicates a 'melanocytic nevus' classification, while absence indicates 'non-melanocytic nevus' (high-risk). The primary metric is mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5) for detection.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Benign Nevus vs. High-Risk/Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The detection output is used in a 'reverse-exclusion' strategy to classify lesions as low-risk (benign) or high-risk (requiring biopsy). The false-negative rate (FNR) for melanoma detection is reported as 0.27%.

datasets_used.name: Multi-centre Benign Melanocytic Nevus Dataset
datasets_used.description: A primary training dataset of 2,040 dermoscopic images of benign melanocytic nevi collected from three tertiary Grade-A hospitals (First Affiliated Hospital of Anhui Medical University: Changjiang Road Campus, High-Tech Zone Campus, and Northern Campus) in mainland China between 2018 and 2024. Images were acquired using DermLite DL4 + HD camera with 10–20x polarized dermoscopy. Gold standard was established via clinical double-blind assessment with expert adjudication.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv10-based detection model.

datasets_used.name: Independent External Test Set (Biopsy-Proven Melanomas)
datasets_used.description: An independent test set of 365 dermoscopic images of early-stage melanomas. These images were collected from the same medical institution as the training data but from a demographically distinct patient cohort. All cases were biopsy-proven (pathology gold standard) and anonymized. Used to evaluate the false-negative rate of the model.

use.testing: Used exclusively as an independent test set to evaluate the false-negative rate (FNR) for melanoma detection.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate confidence intervals for detection metrics (mAP@0.5, mAP@0.95) across validation folds.
To compute the 95% confidence intervals (mean ± t₉,0.975 · SD/√10) for the metrics derived from the 10 validation folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparison between the model and dermatologists. While the text states the model's false-negative rate was 'significantly lower' than that of dermatologists, it does not provide the statistical test, p-value, or alpha threshold used to establish this significance. The only reported statistical method is the calculation of 95% confidence intervals using a t-distribution for the model's detection metrics.

Multi-centre Benign Melanocytic Nevus Da.description: Results from the primary dataset of 2,040 benign nevi, evaluated via patient-level stratified 10-fold cross-validation. Metrics include mAP@0.5, mAP@0.95, Precision (P), Recall (R), Model Size (Wsz), and Latency.
Multi-centre Benign Melanocytic Nevus Da.notes: The full model achieved the highest detection performance. The 95% confidence intervals were calculated using a t-distribution (df=9) on the 10 validation folds.

Comparative Baselines (on Benign Nevus D.description: Performance of other object detection models evaluated on the same benign nevus dataset.
Comparative Baselines (on Benign Nevus D.notes: The Full Model outperformed all listed baselines in mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.95.

Independent External Test Set (Biopsy-Pr.description: Evaluation of the False-Negative Rate (FNR) on an independent set of 365 biopsy-proven melanomas to assess the 'reverse-exclusion' safety strategy.
Independent External Test Set (Biopsy-Pr.notes: The model's FNR was significantly lower than that of dermatologists using either scoring system (McNemar p < 0.001). The model missed only one melanoma among 365 cases.

architecture_details.backbone: PP-LCNet (replacing CSPDarknet-Tiny; includes Stem Conv, multi-stage depthwise-separable convolutions, 5x5 large-kernel convolutions at the tail, H-Swish activation, Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules, and 1x1 convolution with 1,280 channels after global average pooling)
architecture_details.neck: Multiscale Contextual Attention (MCA) modified PANet (replacing standard PANet; uses parallel average, standard-deviation, and max pooling for squeeze phase, and 1xk grouped convolutions for excitation phase to generate multiscale feature maps P3, P4, P5)
architecture_details.head: Decoupled v10Detect Head (three-branch structure: classification, regression, and objectness)
architecture_details.loss_function: Shape-IoU (replacing CIoU for boundary regression; optimizes aspect ratio, centroid position, and border curvature)

training_details.epochs: 100 (with early stopping if validation mAP@0.5 shows no improvement for 15 epochs)
training_details.data_preprocessing: Normalization to [0, 1], channel-wise standardization using ImageNet mean (0.485, 0.456, 0.406) and standard deviation (0.229, 0.224, 0.225)

classification_or_output_head.nms: Disabled for YOLO variants to maintain end-to-end properties

preprocessing_techniques: Channel-wise standardization using ImageNet mean (0.485, 0.456, 0.406) and standard deviation (0.229, 0.224, 0.225)
Quality control using Unsharp Masking Index (UQI > 0.95) and Natural Image Quality Evaluator (NIQE < 5.0)
Hair occlusion screening using the DullRazor algorithm followed by manual review

Data augmentation: Horizontal flip (p=0.5), Vertical flip (p=0.3), Random scaling (0.5–1.2x), HSV jitter (H ± 20%, S ± 30%, V ± 30%), Gaussian noise (σ = 0.1), Random rectangular occlusion (Area ≤ 20%, AR 0.5–2)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Probability p = 0.5; used to eliminate left-right orientation bias.
Probability p = 0.3; used to adapt to varying capture angles.
Range 0.5–1.2x; used to expand size distribution.
H ± 20%, S ± 30%, V ± 30%; used to resist lighting and color temperature variations.
Standard deviation σ = 0.1; used to simulate sensor noise.
Area ≤ 20%, Aspect Ratio 0.5–2; used to replicate hair and glare artifacts.

conclusions: The proposed 'benign-first, reverse-exclusion' strategy using an enhanced YOLOv10 model achieves a high detection mAP@0.5 of 97.69% for benign melanocytic nevi and a very low false-negative rate (FNR) of 0.27% for melanomas.

The model's melanoma false-negative rate (0.27%) is significantly lower than that of dermatologists using the ABCDE rule (11.23%) and the 7-point checklist (12.60%), with statistical significance (McNemar p < 0.001).

The enhanced model maintains a compact size (5.37 MB) and low inference latency (32.02 ms), making it suitable for real-time, on-device screening and potential deployment on smartphones for primary care settings.

The 'benign-first' approach offers a high-precision, low-risk pathway for early melanoma screening in Chinese East-Asian clinical settings by markedly reducing unnecessary biopsies while keeping the miss rate below the clinical safety ceiling of 0.5%.

Ablation studies confirm that the combination of the PP-LCNet backbone, Multiscale Contextual Attention (MCA) neck, and Shape-IoU loss function provides complementary benefits, with each module substantially boosting detection performance individually.
The model demonstrates robustness with rapid loss convergence and stable precision-recall plateaus, indicating no significant overfitting or underfitting during training.

The study is limited to Chinese East-Asian patients and a specific multi-centre dataset from three tertiary hospitals in mainland China, suggesting that generalization to other ethnicities or broader clinical environments requires further validation.
important_remarks: The study population is exclusively East-Asian patients with Fitzpatrick skin types II–III; generalization to other ethnicities or skin tones requires external validation.
The dataset is limited to images collected from three tertiary hospitals in mainland China between 2018 and 2024, potentially introducing geographic and institutional bias.
The external test set consists of only 365 biopsy-proven melanomas, which limits the statistical power for estimating the false-negative rate.
The study lacks long-term follow-up data (≥5 years) to assess melanoma recurrence or validate the long-term safety of the reverse-exclusion strategy.
The comparison with dermatologists used a double-blind, two-round protocol with a four-week washout period, but the specific dermatologists' experience levels beyond '≥10 years' are not detailed.
The model's performance on non-melanocytic lesions (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, haemangioma) was not experimentally evaluated, despite their mention in the introduction.
Regulatory hurdles such as CE marking, FDA clearance, and explainability requirements are noted as necessary for clinical deployment but not addressed in the current study.
The 'benign-first' strategy relies on strict morphological criteria (symmetry, uniform pigmentation, sharp borders), which may not capture all early melanomas that deviate from these features.
